diagrams.title: Computational Complexity Classes Relationship
diagrams.description: A Venn/Euler diagram showing the hierarchical relationship between P, NP, NP-Complete, and NP-Hard complexity classes. P is shown as a proper subset of NP, NP-Complete is shown at the intersection of NP and NP-Hard, and NP-Hard encompasses all three classes outside of NP.
elements: Large outer circle labeled 'NP-Hard' encompassing all other regions
Smaller circle labeled 'NP-Complete' at the intersection of NP and NP-Hard
Smallest innermost circle labeled 'P' inside NP-Complete

Legend or labels indicating: P = problems solvable in polynomial time, NP = problems verifiable in polynomial time, NP-Complete = hardest problems in NP, NP-Hard = at least as hard as NP-Complete problems
diagrams.purpose: Visualize the fundamental containment relationships between P, NP, NP-Complete, and NP-Hard complexity classes to understand how they relate to each other

diagrams.title: Polynomial Time Reduction Flow
diagrams.description: A directed acyclic graph showing how NP-Complete problems relate through polynomial time reductions, demonstrating that if any NP-Complete problem can be solved in polynomial time, all NP problems can be solved in polynomial time.
elements: Branch nodes representing famous NP-Complete problems: SAT, 3-SAT, Vertex Cover, Hamiltonian Cycle, Traveling Salesman, Knapsack, Clique
Directed edges with '≤p' (polynomial time reducible) labels connecting problems
Arrow showing 'If P = NP, all these collapse' concept
Color coding: all NP-Complete problems in same color to show equivalence class
diagrams.purpose: Illustrate the reduction relationships between NP-Complete problems and the concept that they are all polynomially equivalent in difficulty

diagrams.title: P vs NP Question Visualization
diagrams.description: A split visualization showing two scenarios: (1) P ≠ NP where the classes remain distinct, and (2) P = NP where all classes merge. This helps conceptualize what the P vs NP question actually means.
elements: Left diagram: P ≠ NP case showing P as proper subset of NP-Complete within NP, with NP-Hard outside
Right diagram: P = NP case showing all classes merging into one region
Question mark icon at center between diagrams
Labels explaining implications: cryptography collapse, algorithm breakthroughs, etc.
Timeline or notation showing this as 'the greatest unsolved problem in computer science'
diagrams.purpose: Help students understand what the P vs NP question actually asks and the profound implications of either answer
